# About Integrative Health

## What is integrative health?

Integrative health is an approach to health care that focuses on the well-being of the entire person, rather than just treating specific symptoms or diseases. It encompasses physical, mental, emotional, social and spiritual health.   
‌  
**Key Principles of Integrative Health:**

Integrative health aims to empower patients to take an active role in their health, prevent disease and improve overall well-being through a combination of conventional and complementary therapies.

The Wheel of Health illustrates our integrative approach to healing and wellness. It shows the connections between health and other areas of your life.

Our specialists are committed to understanding your unique circumstances. We help you choose treatments, practices and skills to heal and live well.

While integrative medicine embraces modern and traditional approaches to patient care, it ensures patient safety and treatment efficacy by always using evidence-based medicine and therapeutic approaches. This degree program combines three Graduate Certificates — Fundamentals of Integrative Healthcare, Innovation and Entrepreneurship in Healthcare, and Integrative Health Coaching — to prepare students to take their careers to the next level by meeting the rising demand for patient-centered care that focuses on the mind, body, and spirit.

FULL CONTENT:
[Nursing & Public Health](https://triangle.indwes.edu/tag/nursing-public-health/)

# Foundations of Integrative Health: Bridging Traditional and Holistic Approaches

#### [Brittany Shigley](/author/brittany/)

— 8 min read

A study from the [National Institutes of Health](https://www.nih.gov/news-events/news-releases/nih-analysis-reveals-significant-rise-use-complementary-health-approaches-especially-pain-management?ref=triangle.indwes.edu#:~:text=Researchers%20utilized%20data%20from%20the,2002%20to%2049.2%25%20in%202022.) revealed a significant increase in the use of complementary and integrative healthcare approaches since 2002. The [demand for patient-centered](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8201906/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu), customized healthcare is growing, and the healthcare industry is pivoting in response — developing new approaches to medicine that are increasingly collaborative and designed to bridge the gap between modern treatments and traditional wellness practices. An innovative approach to healthcare, integrative medicine recognizes and reflects the unique and wide-ranging needs of each patient by combining conventional and complementary medicine to create personalized patient care.

## Understanding Integrative Health

Integrative health is a comprehensive approach to healthcare that combines conventional medical treatments with complementary and alternative therapies. It focuses on treating the whole person, addressing the mind, body, and spirit, and emphasizing personalized, patient-centered care. When analyzing and understanding the core principles and key components of integrative health, individuals can gain a deeper appreciation for this holistic and evolving field of medicine.

### Definition and Key Components

[Integrative medicine](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1119398/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) is a comprehensive approach to healthcare. It takes a functional approach to diagnosis and combines modern, conventional treatments with more traditional complementary therapies and holistic healing methods. Integrative wellness is achieved by treating the whole patient with a focus on the wellness of the mind, body, and spirit. Integrative medicine relies on teams of collaborative healthcare practitioners to create personalized, patient-based treatment plans designed to address underlying causes of illness, relieve pain, prevent illness, and support optimal health and healing.

### Why It Matters

[Integrative medicine](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6173273/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu#:~:text=The%20increasing%20popularity%20of%20integrative,combining%20alternative%20with%20conventional%20medicine.) recognizes and honors the mind-body connection by supporting mind-body wellness. True integrative wellness aims to improve health and reduce suffering through an optimal combination of conventional treatments and alternative therapies that focus on the whole patient. The goals of integrative wellness are to improve patient care and achieve better patient outcomes.

## The Core Principles of Integrative Health

Integrative health is guided by several core principles that distinguish it from conventional medicine. These principles emphasize a holistic approach to wellness, patient-centered care, and the integration of evidence-based practices. Understanding these foundational tenets is essential for grasping the true nature of integrative health and its potential benefits.

### 1. Holistic Approach to Wellness

[Holistic healthcare](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1273970/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) offers a multidimensional approach to wellness by considering several aspects of an individual's health and wellness. Healthcare professionals in holistic health practices consider the biological, genetic, risk-based, environmental, psychological, spiritual, ethical, familial, and societal dimensions of an individual's health and ailments. This approach acknowledges the uniqueness of each patient, the relationship the patient has with their care team, and the patient's responsibility for their health.

### 2. Focus on Self-Care and Patient-Centered Care

Integrative medicine emphasizes personalized care that supports the patient's role in their own wellness journey through [self-care](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9868272/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu). Integrative medicine facilitates patient empowerment through therapeutic partnerships where patients are encouraged to take control of their health and well-being.

Doctors and therapeutic providers offer patient education that enables individuals to take active roles in their health and well-being through participating in wellness regimens outside the doctor's office. This might involve making lifestyle modifications, eating a balanced diet, following mindful practices, and exercising regularly.

### 3. Striving for Wellness and Prevention

[Preventative care](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10781916/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu#:~:text=Benjamin%20Franklin%20famously%20advised%20fire,worth%20a%20pound%20of%20cure.) has always been considered a superior approach to healthcare. Integrative medicine aims to optimize patient health with a focus on disease prevention, chronic disease management, and integrative pain management while addressing the underlying causes of health issues and balancing the many dimensions of wellness. The well-rounded treatment approach of integrative medicine helps to support the function of the body's systems and natural healing processes to promote self-healing and overall wellness.

### 4. Evidence-Based Medicine

While integrative medicine embraces modern and traditional approaches to patient care, it ensures patient safety and treatment efficacy by always using [evidence-based medicine](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470182/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu#:~:text=Definition/Introduction,%5D%5B2%5D%5B3%5D) and therapeutic approaches. This means that integrative medicine providers rely on treatments that have been scientifically demonstrated as safe and effective for the intended treatment application.

## Evidence-Based Conventional Medicine Paired With Traditional Practices

Integrative health emphasizes the importance of combining evidence-based conventional medicine with traditional practices. This approach seeks to leverage the strengths of both modern healthcare and time-honored therapies to provide comprehensive and effective patient care.

### The Role of Modern Healthcare (Allopathic Medicine)

Also known as contemporary, conventional, or mainstream medicine, [allopathic medicine](https://www.cancer.gov/publications/dictionaries/cancer-terms/def/allopathic-medicine?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) uses FDA-approved pharmaceuticals, surgery, and advanced diagnostics to detect, diagnose, and treat disease in addition to alleviating symptoms.

### Integration With Traditional and Complementary Therapies

While recognizing the power of allopathic treatments, integrative medicine leverages the benefits of combining conventional treatments (pharmaceuticals and surgical procedures) with alternative health treatments from both Eastern and Western medicine practices. This provides patients with a more well-rounded method to healthcare that aims at treating and resolving disease while also supporting better general wellness.

### Addressing Underlying Issues With Functional Medicine

[Functional medicine](https://www.ifm.org/functional-medicine?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) is also often incorporated into an integrative techniques to healthcare. It focuses on identifying and addressing the underlying causes of illness to resolve health problems at the source rather than simply masking symptoms with medication.

Since it is focused on systemic and cellular function, functional medicine relies heavily on diagnostic testing, using a range of comprehensive laboratory tests and diagnostic images.

## Complementary and Alternative Therapies

Complementary and alternative therapies play a significant role in integrative health, offering a diverse range of practices that can be used alongside or in place of conventional treatments. These therapies encompass various approaches, from biologically-based practices to mind-body interventions, and aim to promote overall wellness and address specific health concerns.

### Common Methods and Modalities

Integrative medicine incorporates common alternative and [complementary therapies](https://www.mayoclinic.org/tests-procedures/complementary-alternative-medicine/about/pac-20393581?ref=triangle.indwes.edu). These include practices like acupuncture and massage therapy, as well as ayurvedic therapies, mindfulness meditation, and more.

[Complementary and alternative therapies](https://www.cancer.gov/about-cancer/treatment/cam?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) generally fall into a handful of different categories, including:

* **Biologically-Based Practices** - Uses whole foods and natural supplements like vitamins, botanicals, or special diets to support health and healing.
* **Manipulative Body-Based Practices** - These therapeutic modalities involve working with the body to promote wellness. Examples include reflexology, massage, and chiropractic adjustment.
* **Mind-Body Therapies** - These approaches focus on balancing and syncing the body and mind through breathwork, movement, and relaxation.
* **Energy Healing Approaches** - Based on the belief that energy flows through the body, practices like Reiki and therapeutic touch attempt to balance the energy to promote wellness.
* **Whole Medical Systems** - Entire evolved systems of treatment, such as naturopathic medicine and traditional Chinese medicine, provide a variety of treatment protocols designed to promote wellness and address various ailments.

### Evaluating Safety and Efficacy

Many traditional and alternative therapies have ample anecdotal evidence supporting their efficacy, but many lack high-quality scientific validation. This can lead to a lack of confidence in these modalities and a lack of clarity in how they should be used.

Additionally, [nutritional and herbal supplements](https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/safety?ref=triangle.indwes.edu#:~:text=Safety%20depends%20on%20the%20specific,healthy%20people%20when%20practiced%20appropriately.) are not evaluated by the Food and Drug Administration, which means their ingredients are not always properly disclosed or of the highest quality. These products can also interact with medications, interfering with treatment or sometimes resulting in dangerous reactions.

For these reasons, healthcare practitioners must work closely with their patients in all aspects of their integrative health plans. Trained professionals can help patients identify high-quality products while preventing them from endangering themselves or their wellness plans as a result of drug interactions. Integrative care providers should also work in close collaboration with their patients' complementary therapy providers to ensure that all treatment modalities are safe and provided by a properly trained and licensed practitioner.

## The Benefits of a Blended Model

Integrative medicine's blended approach to healthcare and wellness provides several benefits to patients and healthcare professionals.

### Personalized Health Plan

While healthcare treatment protocols can help provide doctors with an avenue and direction to address a patient's care, medicine should not provide a one-size-fits-all product or service. Personalized health plans consider each patient's unique situation to identify root causes and provide customized, evidence-based treatment and prevention.

### Improved Patient Outcomes

The personalized, [interdisciplinary approach](https://www.ohca.com/article-13-individualized-care-approaches-reflected-care-planning/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) to healthcare that integrative medicine offers results in [superior treatment outcomes](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10823796/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) for patients. With personalized integrative care, patients achieve more notable health improvements with respect to their total well-being, and these results are longer-lasting.

### Positive Patient Experience

Integrative care provides patients with an [elevated treatment experience](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8307064/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu#:~:text=We%20found%20that%20whole%2Dperson%20models%20of%20primary,experience%2C%20clinical%20outcomes%20and%20in%20reducing%20costs.&text=This%20review%20explored%20several%20examples%20of%20such,on%20reduced%20costs%20and%20reduced%20provider%20burnout.). Collaboration across a team of providers streamlines the patient's experience, improving continuity and comprehensiveness of care. Better treatment outcomes achieved through methods that engage and empower the patient to take an active role in their health journey also lead to higher levels of patient satisfaction.

By investing in integrative care that [supports holistic wellness](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK571813/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu#:~:text=Integrated%20care%20has%20been%20defined,team%2Dbased%20approach%20to%20care.), patients ultimately spend less time and money on healthcare. As patients work with their doctors and providers to address current health concerns while improving their overall health to prevent future illness, they feel better physically, mentally, and spiritually.

## Best Practices for Successful Integration

Successfully integrating various methods in healthcare requires careful planning and execution. To maximize the benefits of integrative medicine, certain best practices must be followed to ensure patient safety, treatment effectiveness, and seamless collaboration among providers.

### Effective Communication Among Providers

Successful integrative medicine relies heavily on collaborative care teams. Communication that facilitates the sharing of patient information between authorized providers is essential to providing continuity of care. Effective communication also ensures the safety and efficacy of all prescribed and recommended treatments and lifestyle modifications.

Collaboration and communication can be challenging when multiple parties are involved, and all parties are working within extremely busy schedules. As integrative medicine grows in popularity, more HIPAA-compliant collaboration tools will become available. Additionally, more integrative clinics will be created as the demand for this high standard of care continues to grow.

### Maintaining Scientific Rigor

As the popularity of integrative medicine grows, so does the demand for additional scientific validation of complementary therapies. However, while healthcare professionals await the completion of these vital studies, they must maintain scientific rigor by following research closely to use evidence-based treatment protocols, especially when the safety of an alternative treatment modality or therapy has yet to be demonstrated.

## Advance Your Career in Medicine by Positioning Yourself in the Future of Healthcare

As the demand for customized care increases, the need for healthcare professionals who are well-versed in integrative medicine will grow, too. Graduate students of [Indiana Wesleyan University](https://www.indwes.edu/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) can prepare themselves by studying online to earn a [master's degree in Integrative Health](https://www.indwes.edu/program/ms-integrative-health/?ref=triangle.indwes.edu). This degree program combines three Graduate Certificates — Fundamentals of Integrative Healthcare, Innovation and Entrepreneurship in Healthcare, and Integrative Health Coaching — to prepare students to take their careers to the next level by meeting the rising demand for patient-centered care that focuses on the mind, body, and spirit.

To [learn more about our programs](https://www.indwes.edu/find-your-program?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) and how to begin earning your Master of Science in Integrative Health, we encourage you to [start your journey](https://www.indwes.edu/contact/rfi-online?ref=triangle.indwes.edu) today.

--- SOURCE 4: What is Integrative Medicine? A Comprehensive Guide - AIHCP ---
URL: https://aihcp.net/2024/06/06/what-is-integrative-medicine-a-comprehensive-guide/

SUMMARY:
Integrative medicine is characterized by its emphasis on patient-centered care, integrating evidence-based complementary therapies to optimize health outcomes. This careful integration of complementary and conventional medicine ensures patient safety and enhances health outcomes, distinguishing integrative care from alternative approaches that might rely on less reliable, anecdotal evidence. In conclusion, the trend towards integrative health care is supported by both increased patient demand and the incorporation into mainstream medical practices, with future prospects looking towards expanding this approach amidst challenges that require careful navigation. Healthcare professionals can provide valuable guidance on integrating conventional medical treatments with complementary therapies such as acupuncture, yoga, or herbal supplements, ensuring a coordinated approach that enhances overall health outcomes.

FULL CONTENT:
What is Integrative Medicine? A Comprehensive Guide

By – James M. Katz, BA

In an era where wellness and mental health increasingly intersect with medical care, integrative medicine emerges as a beacon of holistic healing, addressing the full spectrum of individuals’ physical, emotional, and spiritual wellbeing. This approach combines the best of conventional medical practices with a broader understanding of the natural healing processes of the body, making it a pivotal aspect of self-care and wellness strategies. With a growing recognition of its benefits, integrative medicine is reshaping how healthcare is perceived and delivered, marking a significant shift towards a more comprehensive, patient-centered approach to health and healing.



This comprehensive guide delves into the essence of integrative medicine, exploring its core principles and how it diverges from and complements traditional healthcare models. It addresses common modalities employed in integrative care, such as functional medicine, and the importance of evidence-based practice and research in validating these approaches. Additionally, the article examines integrative medicine’s role in managing chronic conditions, its rising popularity, and how individuals can navigate and select suitable integrative health treatments. Through this exploration, readers will gain a deeper understanding of how integrative medicine contributes to a more robust and personalized healthcare paradigm, fostering greater health and wellness outcomes.

Key Takeaways

Defining Integrative Health Care

Integrative health care combines conventional medical practices with complementary approaches in a coordinated manner, focusing on treating the whole person rather than isolated symptoms or diseases. This holistic approach includes a variety of interventions such as medications, physical rehabilitation, psychotherapy, alongside complementary methods like acupuncture, yoga, and probiotics.

Distinction between Conventional, Complementary, and Integrative Health

Conventional medicine, also known as Western medicine, primarily involves scientifically validated methods such as pharmaceutical medications, surgery, and psychotherapy. In contrast, complementary medicine includes treatments like herbal medicine, massage therapy, and acupuncture, which may not always be part of mainstream healthcare but have historical roots and are increasingly used alongside conventional treatments.

Integrative health care is distinct in that it intentionally combines both conventional and complementary approaches to address all aspects of an individual’s health. This coordinated approach aims to enhance the overall quality of life by mitigating the side effects of conventional treatments and emphasizing a comprehensive view of health.

Evolution and Recognition of Integrative Health

The concept of integrative health has evolved significantly over the years, gaining more recognition and implementation within various healthcare settings across the United States.   Research into integrative health continues to explore its benefits in diverse contexts, such as pain management for military personnel and symptom relief for cancer patients.

Historically, integrative medicine has roots in “drugless healing” practices from the early 20th century, contrasting sharply with the allopathic focus on pharmaceuticals. The licensing and educational structures of conventional medicine have aimed at establishing dominance in healthcare, often overshadowing alternative practices. However, the growing acknowledgment of the limitations of a purely scientific approach to health has led to a broader acceptance and integration of holistic and complementary methods.

Integrative medicine’s development is marked by significant milestones such as the establishment of the Academic Consortium for Integrative Medicine and Health,  which includes numerous North American medical institutions that educate about integrative therapies. These institutions play a crucial role in blending conventional medical education with integrative health principles.

Core Principles of Integrative Health Care

Patient-centered Approach

Integrative medicine prioritizes a patient-centered approach, respecting and responding to individual patient preferences, needs, and values. This model ensures that patient values guide all clinical decisions, creating a partnership where patients and their families are actively involved in the decision-making process. The integrative model is healing-oriented, embracing both conventional and complementary therapies to focus on the whole person and lifestyle, not just the physical body.

Incorporation of Mental, Physical, and Spiritual Well-being

The core of integrative medicine is the belief that human beings possess emotional, mental, and spiritual dimensions that are essential in the diagnosis and treatment of disease and the cultivation of wellness. It addresses these dimensions by integrating a variety of therapies and lifestyle changes to treat the whole person—mind, body, and spirit. This approach includes the use of evidence-based practices such as medication, psychotherapy, acupuncture, and yoga, ensuring well-coordinated care among different providers and specialists.

Emphasis on Preventive Care and Wellness

Integrative medicine places a strong emphasis on preventive care and wellness, aiming to improve health outcomes by preventing and managing chronic diseases through lifestyle intervention. This approach supports long-term health by encouraging lifestyle changes, such as personalized dietary plans and exercise programs, tailored to individual health needs. It also integrates various therapies to best meet each person’s unique health needs, thereby optimizing health and ensuring that patients receive the care they want and need.

Common Modalities in Integrative Health Care

Acupuncture

Acupuncture, a key component of Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), is predicated on the belief that the body’s vital energy, qi, flows through specific pathways. Imbalances or blockages in this flow are thought to cause disease. By inserting thin needles at various acupuncture points along these meridians, acupuncturists aim to restore balance and health. Studies have confirmed acupuncture’s efficacy for various conditions such as nausea, dental pain, addiction, and more, making it a valuable modality in integrative health care.

Massage Therapy

Massage therapy is increasingly recognized within integrative health care for its effectiveness across a spectrum of conditions, including chronic pain, anxiety, depression, and post-surgical recovery. By manipulating soft tissues, massage helps relieve pain, reduce stress, and enhance overall well-being.  It is not only a method for relaxation but also a therapeutic intervention widely used in various medical settings to complement other treatments.

Herbal Medicines and Supplements

Herbal supplements, derived from plant sources, are utilized both for treatment and health maintenance. Unlike pharmaceuticals, these supplements are categorized as food by the FDA and are not subject to the same rigorous testing. Consumers are advised to exercise caution, seek professional advice, and opt for reputable brands to ensure safety and efficacy. Herbal remedies can interact with conventional medications, highlighting the need for guided integration into health care.

Mind-body Practices

Mind-body practices such as yoga, tai chi, and meditation are integral to integrative medicine, offering significant benefits for both mental and physical health. These practices help manage stress, improve heart health, and enhance quality of life in those with chronic illnesses. By focusing on the interconnections between mind and body, these modalities promote healing and well-being across various populations and conditions.

The Role of Mental and Emotional Health

Stress Management

Stress management is a crucial aspect of integrative medicine. Techniques such as mindfulness, meditation, and yoga are commonly used to help patients manage stress effectively. These practices not only reduce stress but also promote overall well-being.

Emotional Well-being

Emotional well-being is integral to a person’s overall health. Integrative medicine emphasizes the importance of addressing emotional health through therapies like counseling, cognitive behavioral therapy, and biofeedback. These therapies aim to improve mental resilience and emotional stability.

Mind-Body Techniques

Mind-body techniques are central to integrative medicine. Practices such as guided imagery, progressive muscle relaxation, and biofeedback are used to create a harmonious balance between the mind and body. These techniques help patients achieve inner peace and rejuvenation.



Spirituality in Integrative Medicine

Spiritual Needs

Integrative medicine recognizes that spiritual needs are a vital component of overall health and well-being. Addressing these needs can help patients find meaning, purpose, and connection, which are essential for healing. Practitioners often explore patients’ beliefs and values to tailor treatments that resonate on a deeper level.

Meditation and Mindfulness

Meditation and mindfulness practices are commonly used in integrative medicine to enhance spiritual well-being. These techniques help patients cultivate a sense of inner peace and awareness, which can positively impact their physical and emotional health. Regular practice can lead to reduced stress, improved focus, and a greater sense of balance.

Healing Environments

Creating healing environments is another key aspect of spirituality in integrative medicine. These environments are designed to promote relaxation, comfort, and a sense of safety. Elements such as natural light, soothing colors, and quiet spaces can significantly enhance the healing process. The goal is to create a space where patients feel supported and nurtured.

In integrative medicine, the spiritual dimension is not overlooked but rather embraced as a crucial element of holistic care. This approach ensures that all aspects of a patient’s well-being are addressed, leading to more comprehensive and effective treatment plans.

Evidence-Based Practice and Research

Evidence-based medicine (EBM) is defined as the conscientious, explicit, and prudent application of the best available evidence in making decisions about the care of individual patients. It emphasizes utilizing data from rigorously conducted studies to inform clinical decision-making, thereby enhancing patient care by allowing individuals to choose their preferred healthcare methods while being informed about their safety and efficacy. This approach helps reduce reliance on less reliable, experience-based practices and supports a more scientifically sound basis for medicine.

The Role of Scientific Research in Validating Treatments

Scientific research plays a pivotal role in validating treatments within integrative medicine, ensuring that therapies are both safe and effective. Conventional medicine relies heavily on evidence gathered through rigorous trials and research to substantiate the efficacy of treatments. In contrast, many complementary and alternative treatments lack this level of scrutiny, which can lead to uncertainties about their effectiveness. The integration of complementary and alternative medicine (CAM) with mainstream systems has shown a synergistic effect, improving overall health outcomes through the exchange of evidence-based knowledge.

Examples of Evidence-Supported Modalities

Several modalities within integrative health care have garnered support through scientific research. For instance, acupuncture has been validated for its effectiveness in treating conditions such as nausea and chronic pain, with studies highlighting its role in balancing the body’s vital energy. Similarly, the effectiveness of massage therapy in alleviating pain and reducing stress has been well-documented, making it a valuable component of integrative health care. Additionally, the use of herbal medicines requires careful integration and oversight due to potential interactions with conventional medications, underscoring the importance of evidence-based practice in ensuring safety and efficacy.

The Cochrane Collaboration and researchers like Edzard Ernst emphasize that all medical treatments, whether mainstream or alternative, should adhere to the standards of the scientific method. This rigorous approach is crucial for maintaining the integrity of medical practice and ensuring that all therapeutic practices, regardless of their cultural origin or popularity, are subjected to the same level of scrutiny to protect patient health and safety.

Research and training in traditional medicines are being encouraged globally, reflecting a growing acknowledgment of their potential benefits when integrated properly into medical practice. This has led to an increase in educational initiatives, such as the inclusion of CAM in medical curricula, to equip healthcare professionals with the necessary knowledge to effectively integrate these practices into their treatment protocols.

Integrative vs. Alternative Health Care

Understanding the Differences

Integrative medicine combines mainstream Western medicine with complementary and alternative therapies, focusing on holistic treatment that considers physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual well-being. This approach is distinct from alternative medicine, which is used in place of conventional medicine and often lacks the same level of rigorous scientific validation. Integrative medicine is characterized by its emphasis on patient-centered care, integrating evidence-based complementary therapies to optimize health outcomes. It acknowledges the complex nature of the human body and aims to treat all aspects of an individual’s health, fostering a collaborative relationship between patient and practitioner.

Why Integrative Care is Not a Substitute for Conventional Medicine

Integrative medicine does not advocate substituting proven conventional interventions with unproven therapies. Instead, it aims to complement and enhance conventional medicine by incorporating a variety of evidence-supported modalities such as diet, exercise, and stress management into the treatment plan. This approach ensures that patients benefit from the best of both worlds: the efficacy of conventional medicine and the holistic benefits of complementary therapies. Integrative medicine strives for well-coordinated care among different providers and institutions, ensuring that therapies are not only effective but also tailored to the individual’s needs and preferences.

By maintaining a strong foundation in evidence-based practice, integrative medicine supports the use of complementary therapies that have been scientifically validated,  thus avoiding the risks associated with alternative treatments that may lack empirical support. This careful integration of complementary and conventional medicine ensures patient safety and enhances health outcomes, distinguishing integrative care from alternative approaches that might rely on less reliable, anecdotal evidence.

Integrative Health Care for Chronic Conditions

Integrative health care offers a multifaceted approach to managing chronic conditions, emphasizing the need for treatments that address the whole person rather than just symptoms. Chronic pain, a prevalent chronic condition, often leads individuals to seek complementary therapies. A study in JAMA revealed that many people choose these therapies to align with a mind-body-spirit philosophy and to play an active role in their own healing process.

Managing Chronic Pain

Chronic pain management benefits significantly from integrative therapies. Techniques such as acupuncture have shown efficacy in reducing pain associated with conditions like osteoarthritis and migraine headaches. Additionally, mind-body therapies, including mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR), have demonstrated clinical effectiveness for various chronic conditions, notably in improving pain management and enhancing quality of life. These therapies are often integrated into comprehensive management programs that may include cognitive behavioral methods, relaxation, and biofeedback, providing a holistic approach to pain relief.



Mental Health and Stress Reduction

The impact of chronic conditions on mental health can be profound, making stress reduction an essential component of integrative care. Cognitive behavior therapy (CBT) and mindfulness-based interventions are effective in reducing symptoms of anxiety, depression, and stress associated with chronic illnesses. Techniques like deep breathing, tai chi, and guided imagery are also employed to help individuals manage stress and improve mental health outcomes.

In summary, integrative health care for chronic conditions involves a range of therapies that support physical, mental, and emotional health. By incorporating evidence-based practices and focusing on the patient’s overall well-being, this approach enhances the effectiveness of chronic condition management.

The Growing Trend of Integrative Health Care

Increased Patient Demand

The demand for integrative medicine has seen a significant rise, particularly for managing conditions such as pain, cancer, and more recently, COVID-19. This growing interest is mirrored globally, with substantial portions of populations in both developed and developing nations turning to traditional, complementary, and integrative medicine (TCIM). For instance, in the United States, 42% of the population utilizes some form of TCIM, with similar figures reported across other nations like Australia, France, Canada, and even higher in countries like India and China.

Incorporation in Mainstream Medical Institutions

Integrative health care is increasingly being recognized and incorporated into mainstream medical institutions across the world. This trend is driven by a rising acknowledgment of the limitations of conventional medicine alone to address chronic diseases, which now account for approximately 75% of the US aggregate health care spending. In response, more healthcare settings are integrating TCIM practices into their services, reflecting a shift towards more holistic health approaches that emphasize patient-centered care and multimodal interventions. Additionally, major medical organizations are beginning to include CAM practices in their clinical guidelines, which is a testament to the growing integration of these approaches in conventional healthcare.

Future Prospects and Challenges

The future of integrative health care looks promising but is not without challenges. The field has witnessed a doubling in research publications over the past decade, indicating a surge in scientific interest and validation efforts. However, the quality, safety, and efficacy of TCIM products remain a concern, necessitating continued research and stringent evaluations to ensure their responsible integration into healthcare. Furthermore, the growing trend has stimulated the scientific community and policy organizations globally to delve deeper into the clinical efficacy and policy implications of these therapies.

Simultaneously, the landscape of integrative medicine is evolving with more physicians adopting integrative concepts in their practices. This change is driven by a combination of factors including the holistic approach of treating the root causes of illness, focusing on optimal health and healing, and enhancing the physician-patient relationship. The American Board of Physician Specialties® has also responded by offering board certification in integrative medicine, underscoring the formal recognition and institutional support for this field.

In conclusion, the trend towards integrative health care is supported by both increased patient demand and the incorporation into mainstream medical practices, with future prospects looking towards expanding this approach amidst challenges that require careful navigation.

How to Choose Integrative Health Treatments

Consulting with Healthcare Professionals

When considering integrative health treatments, it is crucial to consult with healthcare professionals who are knowledgeable in both conventional and complementary medicine. This ensures that all aspects of the patient’s health are considered and that treatments are tailored to individual needs. Healthcare professionals can provide valuable guidance on integrating conventional medical treatments with complementary therapies such as acupuncture, yoga, or herbal supplements, ensuring a coordinated approach that enhances overall health outcomes.

Safety and Potential Interactions

One of the primary concerns when choosing integrative health treatments is the safety and potential interactions between different therapies. Patients should be aware that many complementary therapies, like herbal medicines and dietary supplements, can interact with conventional medications, potentially leading to adverse effects.  Therefore, it is important to discuss all current treatments with a healthcare provider to safely incorporate new therapies and avoid harmful interactions.

Finding Credible Resources and Practitioners

Selecting the right practitioner is essential for effective integrative health treatment. Patients should seek professionals who are licensed and have a solid educational background in their field. It is advisable to consult resources like professional organizations, which often provide directories of qualified practitioners. Additionally, when researching health information or treatments, patients should rely on credible sources such as government health agencies or recognized medical institutions to ensure the information is accurate and up-to-date. This approach helps in making informed health decisions and finding reliable practitioners who can deliver high-quality care.

Conclusion

Through the exploration of integrative medicine, it becomes clear that this approach offers a sophisticated blend of conventional and complementary therapies geared towards treating the whole person—body, mind, and spirit. By emphasizing a patient-centered approach, evidence-supported modalities, and the significance of preventive care, integrative health care promises a more holistic and personalized healthcare experience. This comprehensive guide has illustrated how integrative medicine not only complements traditional medical practices but stands as a pivotal force in reshaping the future of healthcare, emphasizing wellness, and the nuanced needs of individuals.

The growing trend toward integrative health care, underscored by a patient-centric philosophy and increasing incorporation into mainstream medical settings,  highlights the evolving landscape of healthcare. As the demand for such holistic treatments rises, so does the need for knowledgeable professionals in the field. For those inspired by the principles and practice of integrative medicine and looking to make a difference, becoming a certified holistic nurse could be your next step toward contributing to this transformative approach to health and healing. This path not only enriches your professional life but also empowers patients to embark on a journey toward comprehensive wellness.

If you’re a registered nurse with a valid RN license to practice then you already meet our prerequisite qualifications to become a certified Holistic Nurse with our organization. We offer a 6-course program, which after completing, leads to becoming a Holistic and Integrative Healthcare Certified Specialist. If you’re interested your next step would be to register for our Holistic Nursing Certification Courses here

FAQs

What Exactly is Integrative Medicine?

Integrative medicine is a medical approach that blends the best of conventional medicine with complementary and alternative therapies. This approach is characterized by the use of a wide range of evidence-based treatments, including those outside of mainstream medicine, to create comprehensive care plans for patients.

Can You Break Down the Five Main Areas of Integrative Medicine?

According to the National Center for Complementary and Integrative Health (NCCIH), integrative medicine encompasses five key domains: 1) manipulative and body-based methods, 2) mind-body medicine, 3) alternative medical systems, 4) energy therapies, and 5) biologically based therapies.

How Credible is Integrative Medicine?

Integrative medicine is a credible field of medicine that combines the most reliable and scientifically supported aspects of conventional and complementary therapies. This approach is tailored to provide the best possible care for each individual, ensuring treatments are both effective and evidence-based.

Is Integrative Medicine Recognized as a Medical Specialty?

Yes, integrative medicine is recognized as a distinct medical specialty. For further details on its evolution as a specialty, or for more information about the American Board of Integrative Medicine (ABOIM), interested parties are encouraged to contact the American Board of Physician Specialties (ABPS), the certifying body responsible for the specialty.

How important is the patient-practitioner relationship in integrative medicine?

In integrative medicine, the relationships between you and your healthcare providers are very important. You are equal partners in your healing process, and maintaining open communication with all your providers helps you get the most out of treatment.

Can integrative medicine help with mental and emotional health?

Yes, integrative medicine believes that your physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual needs affect your health. It uses a combination of therapies and lifestyle changes to address these aspects and improve your overall well-being.

# Bridging the Gap: Integrative Medicine in Modern Clinical Practice

July 29, 2024

9
min. read

![A person doing yoga in warrior pose on a green mat in a bright bedroom.](https://www.medbridge.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=cover,quality=90,format=webp,upscale=false,gravity=0.53x0.47,zoom=1,width=576,height=432/https://mkt3.medbridge.com/posts/bridging-the-gap-integrative-medicine-in-modern-clinical-practice/2024-07-22_Marketing_Bridging-the-Gap-Integrative-Medicine-in-Modern-Clinical-Practice-Article_Blog-Hero_775x225-1.jpg)

## Author

![A smiling person with wavy gray hair and a turquoise shirt appears against a dark background.](https://www.medbridge.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=cover,quality=90,format=webp,upscale=false,zoom=1,width=150,height=150/https://mkt3.medbridge.com/people/cheryl-van-demark.jpeg)

Cheryl Van Demark

PT, MA, C-IAYT

## Categories

## Subscribe to Our Newsletter

As a health professional, do you incorporate integrative approaches into your clinical practice? A recent report from Harvard Health indicates that about two-thirds of Americans aged 50 to 80 now use some form of integrative medicine for their health and well-being.1

Integrative medicine first emerged in the U.S. in the 1930s through the efforts of medical practitioners called "drugless healers." These practitioners, including naturopaths, chiropractors, midwives, herbalists, shamans, indigenous medicine practitioners, and others, were marginalized after the 1910 Flexner Report, which called for the standardization of medical education and led to the exclusion of many alternative practices.2

In this article, we will explore the historical origins, modern definitions, clinical uses, community support, guiding principles, and challenges and opportunities of integrative medicine.

## **Origins and Evolution of Integrative Medicine**

The term "drugless healers" is historically associated with a 19th-century Germanic philosophy centered around a "nature cure" that embraced the body's inherent drive to maintain health. This drive was regarded as an intelligent "vital force," considered divine by some, permeating every atom, molecule, and cell. The philosophy emphasized natural remedies and holistic approaches, contrasting sharply with the emerging pharmaceutical and surgical practices of the time. This vital force was seen as capable of re-establishing homeostatic conditions to promote healing.3

Fast forward to the present, the [Andrew Weil Center for Integrative Medicine](https://awcim.arizona.edu/) defines integrative medicine (IM) as "healing-oriented medicine that takes account of the whole person, including all aspects of lifestyle. It emphasizes the therapeutic relationship between practitioner and patient, is informed by evidence, and makes use of all appropriate therapies."4

To further elaborate on this modern approach, the Center outlines eight defining principles. These principles include openness to new paradigms founded in good science and the prioritization of illness prevention and health promotion. Uniquely and critically, the last principle states, "Practitioners of integrative medicine should exemplify its principles and commit themselves to self-exploration and self-development."

## **Integrative Medicine in Clinical Practice**

As a health professional who has incorporated integrative medicine into my practice, I was inspired to do so through self-exploration and engaging in contemplative practices. These practices can take many forms, both ancient and modern, and often involve mindful movement followed by being still, which helps calm the mind.

Regularly practicing these activities can draw our attention to our inner experiences. Some people even describe becoming more aware of their internal sensations or vital energy, while others experience spontaneous insights, inspirations, or a boost in creativity.

The integration of these practices into clinical settings can be highly beneficial:

Incorporating acupuncture and mindfulness practices for pain management.

Utilizing yoga therapy to support all aspects of a biopsychosocial-spiritual approach to self-care for individuals with chronic conditions.

Employing contemplative practices featuring mindful movement to assist with nervous system self-regulation to [reduce stress](/blog/managing-stress-and-anxiety-is-as-easy-as-abc/) and enhance patient outcomes.

Evidence-based research supports these approaches, showing significant benefits in various health conditions. For instance, acupuncture can effectively reduce chronic pain, yoga therapy can alleviate symptoms of depression and anxiety, and mindfulness meditation can enhance mental health and reduce stress.

## **Building Community and Professional Support**

Community and professional support are crucial for promoting and sustaining integrative medicine practices. Engaging in community activities and professional networks can provide the encouragement and resources necessary to embrace a more integrative form of healthcare.

Medbridge has supported several courses that feature [integrative approaches to rehabilitation](/course-catalog/details/integrative-treatment-for-patients-experiencing-chronic-pain-cheryl-van-demark-pt-geriatrics/). These courses have been instrumental in providing training and resources for health professionals looking to incorporate integrative practices into their work. Additionally, networking with colleagues on a similar path is invaluable for professional growth and development.

For instance, conferences offered by the International Association of Yoga Therapists bring together health professionals from various disciplines to discuss and share insights on integrative practices. The science supporting mindfulness, meditation, and yoga for autonomic nervous system regulation has been creating significant connections with Western medicine.

The [American Holistic Health Association](https://ahha.org/) and the [Academy of Integrative Health & Medicine](https://aihm.org/) have also played key roles in promoting integrative practices through professional development, research, and advocacy. These organizations offer resources, training, and platforms for practitioners to share experiences and learn from each other.

## **Guiding Principles for Integrative Physical Therapy**

Integrative medicine can significantly impact the field of physical therapy, providing a more holistic approach to patient care. A key publication that has contributed to the field is the [Guiding Principles for the Practice of Integrative Physical Therapy](https://academic.oup.com/ptj/article/doi/10.1093/ptj/pzad138/7304129?login=false), co-authored by a team of experts in the Physical Therapy Journal (PTJ), including myself.5

Lead author, Catherine Justice, envisioned starting a conversation within the APTA to explore whether integrative physical therapy would become yet another facet of specialization or possibly shape how future professionals are educated. Such a conversation seems timely, given that physical rehabilitation and surgery remain some of the last disciplines to embrace an integrative medicine perspective.6

This open-access PTJ article outlines five guiding principles that form the foundation for integrating physical therapy into broader healthcare practices. Here are the key principles:

[caption id="attachment\_17139" align="aligncenter" width="292"] Image copyright Catherine Justice, used with permission.[/caption]

![Venn diagram with circles for Therapeutic Partnership, Salutogenesis, Movement as an Integrative Experience, Living Systems Theory, and Whole Person Health featured in medbridge.](https://www.medbridge.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=cover,quality=90,format=webp,upscale=false,zoom=1,width=234,height=225/https://mkt3.medbridge.com/posts/bridging-the-gap-integrative-medicine-in-modern-clinical-practice/Integrative-Physical-Therapy-234x225.jpeg)

**Therapeutic Partnership**: Focuses on building a collaborative and trust-based relationship between the patient and practitioner, emphasizing mutual respect and shared decision-making.

**Living Systems Theory**: Integrates the understanding that the human body is a complex, dynamic system. It encourages treatment approaches that recognize and work with this complexity to support overall health.

**Whole Person Health**: Emphasizes the importance of addressing the physical, emotional, and social aspects of a patient's health. It promotes a comprehensive approach to treatment that goes beyond symptom management.

**Movement as an Integrative Experience**: Recognizes the therapeutic potential of movement and encourages incorporating various movement-based therapies to enhance physical and mental well-being.

**Salutogenesis**: Focuses on factors that support human health and well-being, rather than on factors that cause disease. It promotes a positive approach to health by identifying and enhancing factors that contribute to a person's overall wellness.

Reflecting on how little the innate human capacity for healing is emphasized in today's medical practice, it is vital to consider the unique individuality and environment of each patient in their plan of care. Enhancing interprofessional communication and positioning the patient at the center of the care circle are essential steps toward more holistic and effective healthcare.

## **Overcoming Challenges and Embracing Opportunities in Integrative Medicine**

Long-term opioid treatment in allopathic medicine remains prevalent despite its problems. Non-pharmacologic options for persistent pain, as recommended in guidelines such as the 2016 CDC Guideline for Prescribing Opioids for Chronic Pain, remain limited. A growing tide of interventional pain medicine, spine surgery, and joint replacement is simply not economically sustainable.6

Integrative medicine offers promising alternatives to traditional practices. For example, acupuncture, yoga, and mindfulness practices have shown efficacy in managing chronic pain and reducing reliance on opioids. By incorporating these methods, healthcare systems can provide more sustainable and holistic care options.

Incorporating these integrative practices is particularly relevant in places like Arizona, where a significant portion of the population is older adults with chronic pain and chronic illness. According to the CDC, between 2019 and 2021, chronic pain was present in 21 percent of the U.S. population.7 This is rapidly approaching rates of cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes.

Given the rising rates of chronic pain and the associated economic burden, adopting integrative practices such as acupuncture, yoga, and mindfulness can help address these issues more effectively and provide patients with sustainable, holistic care options.

## **An Integrative Path Forward**

Integrative medicine offers a holistic approach that considers the whole person, including lifestyle, therapeutic relationships, and evidence-based therapies. It emphasizes self-exploration and development for practitioners, creating a more inclusive and effective healthcare practice. By embracing these principles and fostering community support, we can bridge the gap between traditional and integrative practices, ultimately enhancing patient care and outcomes.

### Meet the Author

![A smiling person with wavy gray hair and a turquoise shirt appears against a dark background.](https://www.medbridge.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=cover,quality=90,format=webp,upscale=false,zoom=1,width=150,height=150/https://mkt3.medbridge.com/people/cheryl-van-demark.jpeg)

[Cheryl Van Demark, PT, MA, C-IAYT](https://www.medbridge.com/educate/instructors/cheryl-vandemark-pt-geriatrics)

Cheryl Van Demark is a physical therapist, yoga therapist, and yoga teacher with a master's degree in physical education and exercise science. She is approaching 40 years of helping individuals optimize body alignment, restore movement, build strength, and cultivate a balance in body, mind, and spirit to pursue joyful living. Cheryl is deeply inspired to integrate the wisdom of yoga into the future of healthcare.

Cheryl established Health In Motion LLC in 2009 to provide integrative therapeutic services. She codeveloped and is the primary facilitator for the Chronic Pain Self-Care Program within the Dignity Health Yavapai Regional Medical Center. She provides professional development on topics of health creation and resilience for clinicians in rehabilitation medicine for various Arizona-based healthcare organizations. Cheryl also provides public yoga instruction through Yavapai College and is adjunct faculty for the Prescott College Yoga Teacher Training Program. Her experience as a physical therapist intelligently informs her approach to yoga practice.

Cheryl received her BS in physical therapy in 1983, her MA in physical education through Northern Arizona University in 1991, yoga teacher training in 2008, yoga therapist education in 2010, and a Reiki Master designation in 2016 through the Usui lineage. She also completed an integrative health and lifestyle program with the University of Arizona in 2014. "C-IAYT" designates certification with the International Association of Yoga Therapists as a yoga therapist, and Cheryl served as faculty for a number of years with the Integrative Yoga Therapy Program. She also greatly enjoys writing and has contributed book chapters to Integrative Rehabilitation Practice: The Foundations of Whole-Person Care for Health Professionals and Fostering Creativity in Rehabilitation. She is a member of the Yoga Alliance, the International Association of Yoga Therapists, the American Physical Therapy Association, and the International Association for the Study of Pain.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Integrative medicine emphasizes the importance of addressing all aspects of a person's health—physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual—creating a comprehensive approach to wellness.
This approach fosters strong partnerships between patients and healthcare providers, empowering individuals to take an active role in their treatment decisions and health outcomes.
Integrative medicine combines conventional medical treatments with complementary therapies, ensuring a holistic approach that leverages the strengths of both modalities for optimal patient care.
A core principle of integrative medicine is focusing on disease prevention and promoting overall wellness through lifestyle changes, early intervention, and education for sustained health benefits.
